intro: 1998. Marvel just clawed out of bankruptcy. You own the greatest library of characters in entertainment — Spider-Man, the X-Men, Iron Man — and almost no cash. Hollywood thinks comic-book movies are a joke. You run Marvel, and you're going to prove them wrong the hard way.

realOutcome.valuation: sold to Disney for $4B; the MCU has grossed $30B+
realOutcome.summary: Marvel went from bankruptcy to inventing the biggest franchise in film history. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: You're broke and need cash immediately. Studios will pay upfront to license your characters — Fox wants the X-Men, Sony wants Spider-Man — but the terms are lopsided in their favor.

choices.text: License characters to studios to survive.
choices.reveal: Marvel licensed its heroes — X-Men to Fox, Spider-Man to Sony — for upfront cash and small royalties. The deals were lopsided, but they kept the lights on and proved the characters could carry blockbusters.

choices.text: Hold all the rights and wait for a better day.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect the IP and run out of money defending it. A pristine character library owned by a dead company is worth nothing to anyone.

choices.text: Sell the characters outright for a lump sum.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You sell the crown jewels for survival cash. Someone else builds the cinematic universe on the heroes that should have been yours.

decisions.scenario: X-Men and Spider-Man become giant hits — for the studios. You collect a tiny royalty while others make hundreds of millions on your characters. The math is infuriating.

choices.text: Realize licensing leaves all the upside on the table.
choices.reveal: Marvel watched studios earn fortunes on its characters while it took a sliver. That painful realization set up the boldest move in the company's history.

choices.text: Keep licensing everything cheaply for safe cash.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You stay a low-risk licensing shop forever, collecting crumbs while others feast. Safe, small, and permanently on the sidelines of your own success.

choices.text: Sue the studios to renegotiate the old deals.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You spend years and millions in court instead of building. Even if you win, you've made every future partner wary of dealing with you.

decisions.scenario: You want to make your own films and keep the profits. To do it, you'd take a $525M loan from Merrill Lynch, using your remaining characters — including Iron Man — as collateral. If a movie flops, you could lose them.

choices.text: Take the risky loan and finance your own movies.
choices.reveal: Marvel took the famous $525M Merrill Lynch loan, pledging characters like Iron Man as collateral, to self-finance its own films. Betting the characters on itself was the whole ballgame.

choices.text: Stay a licensor and never risk the characters.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You play it safe and never control your own films. Marvel Studios, and the $30B universe it builds, simply never exists.

choices.text: Sell to a big studio now while the IP is hot.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cash out before proving you can make the movies yourself — and leave almost all the value on the table for the buyer.

decisions.scenario: Your first self-made film needs a hero and a star. The safe pick is a famous A-lister and a household-name character. Your team wants to lead with Iron Man — a B-list hero — played by Robert Downey Jr., an actor most studios consider too risky to insure.

choices.text: Cast Robert Downey Jr. and lead with Iron Man.
choices.reveal: Marvel bet its first film on a B-list hero and a high-risk actor. Iron Man (2008) was a massive hit, RDJ became the face of a franchise, and the post-credits scene launched a shared universe.

choices.text: Lead with a safe, famous flagship hero instead.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A generic 'safe' launch gets a generic result. Without RDJ's spark and the audacity of starting with Iron Man, there's no charm and no cultural moment.

choices.text: Cast a cheaper, 'safer' actor to reduce risk.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You save on the star and lose the soul. The exact chemistry that made Iron Man work — and made the whole universe possible — never happens.

decisions.scenario: Iron Man worked. The universe is taking off, and Disney comes calling with a $4B offer to buy the whole company. You could stay independent and keep it all — or take the deal.

choices.reveal: Marvel sold to Disney in 2009 for $4B. Disney's resources and distribution turbocharged the MCU into the highest-grossing franchise in film history — a spectacular outcome for a company that had been bankrupt a decade earlier.

choices.text: Stay independent and keep all the upside.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You keep control but lack Disney's global machine. The MCU grows, but slower and smaller, and you carry all the risk of every expensive bet alone.

choices.text: Sell to a rival studio for less, faster.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take a quicker, cheaper exit to a buyer without Disney's reach. The characters end up managed, not unleashed.

bands.line: From bankruptcy to the biggest franchise in film. Legend episode: WON.
A healthy licensing business — but never the studio that changed cinema.
You gave the heroes away and stayed broke. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Marvel 16x'd by betting on itself. Today's agents flagged a company sitting on assets it's too scared to use →
